Home /echomq/cache/cache-aside-two-layers
EchoMQ / The Cache / Cache-aside, two layers

Module 01 · The Cache

Cache-aside,
two layers

Declare the near-cache, serve reads from ETS, fill from Valkey on a miss, frame every value with its version.

The near-cache is declared, not discovered. Every table registers its full specification in the directory at start — kind, TTL, loader, coherence mode — and a cache absent from the directory does not exist. The read path is cache-aside at ETS speed: a hit stays in the caller’s process, a miss falls through to the L2 Valkey, and if that misses too, the declared loader runs. A write frames every value with its mint-time version — the seed of the coherence that module 03 builds on. All real code, grounded in echo/apps/echo_store.

The framing The three dives Redis Patterns Applied References

The declared near-cache, layer by layer

Pick a component to see its role. The cache is declared at the top; the read path tries each layer in order and stops at the first that answers; the write frames both layers with the same mint-time version. All three components are real: L1 is an ETS table in the caller’s process, L2 is Valkey keyed ecc:{table}:id, and the version is a 14-byte branded id.

A cache that is declared is a cache that can be enumerated, monitored, and governed — the directory is the guarantee that what exists is always knowable.

The three dives

Each dive grounds in the real echo/apps/echo_store code — the directory and the two tiers, then the read path from the kind gate through the L2 frame, then the write that frames both layers with a mint-time version. Follow them in order or start at the one that matters most.

01.1built

Declared, not discovered

The directory, owner monitoring, the ETS tier, the Valkey keyspace, the ecc:{table}:id hashtag.
01.2built

The cache-aside read

fetch/3: the kind gate, the caller-side L1 lookup, the miss path, the three sources :hit | :l2 | :fill.
01.3built

Writing both layers

put/3-4 frames version <> value into L2 + L1; invalidate/3 drops both unconditionally.

Redis Patterns Applied

This module is the depth behind two chapters in Redis Patterns Applied: R1 · Caching — the cache-aside, stampede-prevention, and session patterns this pillar applies in real code; and R5 · Streams & Events — the bus-coherent invalidation that keeps a versioned fill current. There the pattern is the door; here is the near-cache it names.

The version on every row

Every value in this module is framed with a 14-byte mint-time version — the seed of coherence. The third module, Coherence, builds the newer-wins comparison on exactly that version. The manuscript chapter these figures realize is The Branded Component System · the Store.

References

Sources

  • Erlang/OTP — the ets module — the public, read-concurrent L1 table a hit reads directly, in the caller’s process.
  • Valkey — Cluster specification — the {table} hashtag lands every ecc: key on one of 16384 slots.
  • Valkey — GET — the L2 probe a miss issues to find the framed value.
  • Valkey — SET — the write command that places version <> value with PX ttl_ms.
  • Valkey — DEL — the L2 drop used by invalidate/3.
  • Söderqvist — A new hash table (Valkey, 2025) — the L2 the near-cache fronts.
  • Helland — Life Beyond Distributed Transactions — the entity addressed by a key, cached close to its use.
  • King — Announcing Snowflake (2010) — the mint-time id every cached value is framed with.

Related in this course

  • The Cache — the chapter landing for pillar III.
  • The Protocol — the keyspace and branded-id gate the ecc: prefix stands beside.
  • The Queue — the other pillar over the same wire.
  • The Bus — the broadcast wire a coherence message travels over.
  • The Branded Component System · the Store — the manuscript chapter this module realizes.
  • Overview — the three pillars and the system as a whole.
